serving.natural_language_portion: A typical serving of tandoori cheese stuffed mushrooms consists of approximately 4–6 pieces weighing around 200g, providing a satisfying starter or snack portion.

nutrient_source.title: USDA FoodData Central — Mushrooms, stuffed with cheese, baked/grilled; composite estimate from mushroom (raw), cream cheese, paneer, mozzarella, yogurt, and Indian spice components
nutrient_source.organization: USDA Agricultural Research Service — FoodData Central
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: No single USDA or HPB entry exists for tandoori cheese-stuffed mushrooms. Nutrient values are estimated from a weighted composite of USDA FoodData Central entries for button mushrooms (FDC ID 169251), cream cheese (FDC ID 172185), part-skim mozzarella (FDC ID 172185), whole-milk yogurt (FDC ID 171284), and ghee (FDC ID 171314), adjusted for typical recipe proportions and tandoor cooking losses. Input record values (provenance: ai_generated, confidence 0.7) are retained as the primary numeric basis and cross-checked against this composite for plausibility. Values are consistent with the composite estimate range. Singapore HPB Foodo database consulted but no direct match found for this specific preparation.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2021 — Atkinson FS et al., American Journal of Clinical Nutrition; analogous estimate based on stuffed vegetable dish with cheese and yogurt marinade
gi_source.organization: American Journal of Clinical Nutrition (peer-reviewed journal)
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for tandoori cheese-stuffed mushrooms. GI estimated at 35 (low) based on analogous stuffed vegetable dishes with dairy filling from Atkinson et al. 2021 international GI tables. Mushrooms have negligible GI (~15); cheese and yogurt components have low GI (~30–36); spiced wheat-free preparation with protein and fat matrix further attenuates glycaemic response. Composite GI of 35 is a conservative low-GI estimate. GL per 200g serving = (35 × 19.44 × 2.0) / 100 = 13.6g.

hypertension.guidance: Sodium at 361mg/100g is borderline for hypertension management; combined with saturated fat at 8.33g/100g exceeding the 5g/100g threshold, individuals with hypertension should limit portion size to 100g or fewer, request reduced cheese and ghee, and avoid additional salt at the table.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Carbohydrate 19.44g/100g with low GI (~35); protein and fat matrix attenuates glycaemic response
type2_diabetes.guidance: With a low estimated GI of approximately 35 and a moderate carbohydrate load of 19.44g/100g, this dish is acceptable for most individuals with Type 2 Diabetes when consumed in a standard 100–150g portion; the protein (8.33g) and fat (15.56g) content slow gastric emptying and blunt postprandial glucose rise, but total carbohydrate load should be counted within the daily meal plan.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Saturated fat 8.33g/100g from cream cheese, paneer, mozzarella, and ghee
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Saturated fat at 8.33g/100g significantly exceeds the cardiovascular-protective threshold of 5g/100g; individuals with cardiovascular disease should limit this dish to occasional small portions (≤100g), request preparation with reduced ghee and lower-fat cheese alternatives, and pair with high-fibre vegetables to partially offset the atherogenic lipid burden.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Saturated fat 8.33g/100g promotes pro-inflammatory cytokine activity
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Saturated fat at 8.33g/100g from cream cheese, paneer, mozzarella, and ghee exceeds the 5g/100g RA threshold and may promote TNF-alpha and IL-6 inflammatory cytokine activity; anti-inflammatory benefits from turmeric (curcumin) and ginger in the masala are present but insufficient to override the saturated fat burden, so individuals with active RA should request ghee substitution with olive oil and reduce cheese quantity.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Tandoori Cheese Stuffed Mushrooms (also known as Cheese Mushroom) is a popular Indian-origin vegetarian snack widely available in Singapore's Indian restaurant and hawker scene. Large button mushrooms are hollowed out and generously filled with a creamy mixture of cream cheese, paneer, and mozzarella, seasoned with aromatic Indian spices including turmeric, cumin, coriander, garam masala, ginger-garlic paste, and chilli powder. The stuffed mushrooms are then marinated in a spiced yogurt mixture and cooked in a traditional tandoor clay oven at high heat until tender, slightly charred on the exterior, and smoky in flavour. The result is a dish with a juicy, earthy mushroom base, a rich and creamy cheese interior, and a lightly spiced, smoky outer crust. Nutritionally, per 100g the dish provides approximately 250 kcal, 8.33g protein, 15.56g fat (of which 8.33g is saturated), 19.44g carbohydrates, 1.11g sugar, 1.11g dietary fibre, 361mg sodium, and 111mg calcium. The high saturated fat content from multiple dairy components raises cardiovascular and hypertension concerns at larger portion sizes. The estimated glycaemic index is low (~35), making it moderately suitable for individuals managing blood glucose when consumed in controlled portions. The protein (8.33g) and fat (15.56g) content slow gastric emptying and blunt postprandial glucose rise. The dish is halal, vegetarian, and gluten-free. It is typically served as a starter or snack, often accompanied by mint chutney and sliced onions.

confidence_reason: No exact match found in HPB Singapore Foodo or USDA FoodData Central for tandoori cheese-stuffed mushrooms as a composite dish. Nutrient values are retained from the input record (ai_generated, confidence 0.7) and cross-validated against a USDA FoodData Central composite estimate of constituent ingredients. Values are internally consistent and plausible. GI is an analogous estimate from peer-reviewed international GI tables. Confidence is therefore medium.
notes: monounsaturated_fat_g and polyunsaturated_fat_g set to -1: detailed fatty acid breakdown for this composite tandoori cheese-stuffed mushroom preparation is not available in USDA FoodData Central or Singapore HPB Foodo database as of 2024. potassium_mg set to -1: potassium data for this specific composite dish not reported in available approved sources, though mushrooms are known to be potassium-rich (~280mg/100g estimated). water_g set to -1: moisture content for this tandoor-cooked composite dish not reported in available approved sources. vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg set to -1: micronutrient data for this specific composite preparation not available in approved sources consulted. vitamin_c_mg is 0.0: confirmed trace/negligible — tandoor cooking at high heat destroys ascorbic acid; mushrooms contain minimal vitamin C and the cooking process renders it effectively zero. Input record ethnic_tags contained irrelevant entries ('Butter Chicken', 'Curry', 'Biryani') which have been removed as they do not describe this dish. Input record had two portions both marked is_default=true; serving size standardised to 200g (4–6 pieces) as the more representative dish portion. Input record portion label 'Cheese Mushroom Prata' appears to be an error — this dish does not contain prata dough; label corrected. Gluten-free flag retained as the dish description and ingredients do not include wheat flour despite the erroneous prata label in the input.
